title: Emotional Cartography
summary: This episode explore emotional topography and how algorithms like Google Maps shape how we move and emotionally connect to our surroundings.
showNotes: Part of the Supernova podcast series where Master of Media Design students investigate algorithms embedded in our everyday lives.
transcript:
Imagine you standing at an intersection in an unfamiliar part of the city, trying to decide where to go. What would you do? If you thought, I would take a look at Google Maps, you're not alone. More than a billion people use Google Maps every month to locate themselves. In today's episode, we will explore emotional topography, how algorithms like Google Maps shape not just how we move, but how we emotionally connect to our surroundings. This isn't just about finding the fastest route. It's about how we mark places, remember paths, and make a city feel like ours.

We'll hear from everyday users about how they use Google Maps, reflect on stories of dependence and detours, and uncover the emotional layers behind seemingly practical decisions.

"So I think I use Google Maps at least three times a day, so every day, because I use it for different reasons. Like I use it to find my way through the city, but I also use it to see if a store is closed, or where some places that I want to go are. I use it to see the distance to some places that sometimes I think I want to go walking, how far, and how much time I need to do that. It depends on the situation. If I'm going somewhere other than my school, I use it basically all the time.

For example, I had to find a restaurant for dinner with my classmates and I didn't look other than the reviews; I look at the Google reviews if I have to find a dinner place. And it's quite nice that the places with the highest reviews end up being nice places. It gives exact data, like, okay, you go there and you're not disappointed."

Google Maps optimizes our time, making cities more understandable, but also prompting us how we experience them. We depend on it not just for navigating, but for everyday choices, from ordering food, finding a coffee, or planning how we explore a new place.

Tech blogger Justin O'Bain notes that one of the common problems that customers report on maps nowadays is the inability to find the correct entrances to buildings. Such a concern highlights the new level of detail that is now demanded of a map. It is not simply about locating an individual address, but understanding how it works.

Google Maps is now established in the broader digital ecosystem. It has become one of the basic platforms on which other software operations are built. It also introduced a new form of maps that are not only spatial representations, but also temporal entities, based on real-time feedback systems capable of connecting multiple data streams to specific places. Moreover, the consequences of choosing what will be or not be on the map become increasingly complex.

"I love the options to put icons in the maps, like, I have icons for everything that I do. The best hairdressers that I went to, I put an icon. The supermarkets, I have an icon. Work, I have an icon. Home, I have an icon. If someone steals my cell phone, I have my whole life in Google Maps, so they know where to find me."

"I have the few countries that I traveled to. I saved them just to track my timeline of that month or that year. Because when I moved from India to here, it shows me that, okay, this was my August timeline and I have been in two countries and that makes me happy that, okay, I was in two different zones.

So my dream was to be in Iceland and I had an eight-day trip of the coastal Iceland. So I basically saved every stop that we parked. So I want to know that I was there."

Personalizing maps is an act of emotional appropriation, turning abstract spaces into personal geography by choosing what will appear first. A favorite coffee shop or a saved walking route can become a part of our identity and change the way we see ourselves in the space. Luca Ayala and Nevis Maya note that Google Maps project, Tá no Mapa, translated as "It's on the Map," carried out in the favelas of Rio de Janeiro in 2019, revealed the gap between the rich mental maps of favela residents and the spatial simplification of traditional maps. Participants often debated what constituted a point of interest and what kind of information should be recorded. Google Maps is more than just a tool; it is a mirror that reflects our habits, priorities, and emotions.

Emotional topography explores how we imprint feelings, memories, and personal meanings onto maps, turning practical navigation tools into emotional landscapes filled with attachment, nostalgia, and identity. It reveals that our connection to space is not purely functional.

Algorithms may guide us, but the emotional layers we add—our saved places, favorite routes, and detours—are what truly make a place feel like ours. Next time you open Google Maps, think about what your favorite places or paths might say about how you appropriate your space.
